21.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted file transfer. Credentials are sent in plaintext and can be sniffed on the network. Prefer SFTP or SCP instead.

steps: Find 'Microsoft FTP Service' or 'FTP Publishing Service'
Optional: Open 'Turn Windows features on or off' → uncheck IIS → FTP Server
If using proftpd: sudo systemctl stop proftpd && sudo systemctl disable proftpd
Verify: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :21 (should return nothing)
Use SFTP over SSH instead: sftp user@pi-ip

22.dangerous_reason: Remote shell access. Brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks are constant. Weak passwords or exposed keys allow full device control.

steps: Verify: sudo systemctl status ssh (should show 'inactive')
Or: sudo raspi-config → Interface Options → SSH → Disable

23.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted remote shell. Completely obsolete and replaced by SSH. Session hijacking and credential theft are trivial on local networks.

steps: Uncheck 'Telnet Client' and 'Telnet Server' if listed
Find 'Telnet' service → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SSH with key-based login instead

25.dangerous_reason: Mail server port. If this is not your intentional mail server, it is an open-relay risk and a common spam and phishing vector.

steps: Find 'Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP)' or IIS mail services
Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
In IIS Manager, remove any SMTP virtual server if present
If using sendmail: sudo systemctl stop sendmail && sudo systemctl disable sendmail
Home Pis rarely need a mail server — disable unless intentional

53.dangerous_reason: DNS server port. If you are not running a DNS server, this should not be open. Misconfigured DNS is abused in amplification DDoS attacks (CVE-2023-28458 class issues on various DNS software).

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: Server Manager → Remove DNS Server role if installed
If using dnsmasq on Pi as LAN DNS only, bind to local interface — not 0.0.0.0

pi.description: Stop public DNS on Raspberry Pi
steps: Run: sudo systemctl stop bind9 (if installed)
Pi-hole/dnsmasq should listen on LAN IP only, never be port-forwarded
Do not expose port 53 to the internet

80.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted web traffic. Logins and session cookies can be intercepted. If you run a web server, use HTTPS (443) with valid TLS instead.

windows.description: Disable HTTP web server on Windows
steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: Turn Windows features off → uncheck 'Internet Information Services'

linux.description: Stop HTTP web server on Linux
steps: Run: sudo systemctl stop apache2 (or nginx)
Run: sudo systemctl disable apache2 (or nginx)
If web server is needed, configure HTTPS on port 443 only
Run: sudo systemctl stop apache2 (or nginx/lighttpd)
Use HTTPS or restrict to localhost for admin panels
Do not port-forward port 80 from your router

110.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted email retrieval. Credentials and message contents can be intercepted on the LAN. Prefer POP3S (995) or IMAPS (993).

windows.description: Disable POP3 on Windows mail server
steps: Stop any POP3 / mail retrieval service you do not need
If using hMailServer or similar, disable POP3 in the admin console
Stop Dovecot/Courier POP3: sudo systemctl stop dovecot
Or bind mail services to 127.0.0.1 only
Stop mail daemons if installed: sudo systemctl stop dovecot
Prefer encrypted mail (993/995) if you must run a mail server

111.dangerous_reason: RPC portmapper exposes NFS/RPC services for enumeration. Attackers use it to map NFS, mountd, and other RPC endpoints for lateral movement.

steps: Find 'RPC Endpoint Mapper' only if you intentionally run NFS/RPC servers
Prefer blocking inbound 111 in Windows Firewall for untrusted LAN segments
Also stop nfs-server if unused: sudo systemctl stop nfs-server
Only keep NFS/rpcbind if you intentionally share files

135.dangerous_reason: Windows RPC endpoint mapper. Used in lateral movement and remote service abuse. Should not be reachable from untrusted devices.

steps: Create inbound rule: Block TCP 135 from non-trusted IPs
Disable unused remote admin features (Remote Registry, etc.)
This port is typically on Windows endpoints
From the Linux gateway/firewall: block TCP 135 to Windows hosts from guest VLANs
Example: sudo iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp --dport 135 -j DROP

pi.description: Block MSRPC on the Pi firewall path
steps: If scanning a Windows PC, harden that PC instead
Use your router/Pi firewall to isolate IoT from Windows admin ports

139.dangerous_reason: Legacy Windows file sharing. Highly exploitable — used by worms like Conficker and EternalBlue-era lateral movement on flat networks.

steps: Open Settings → Network & Internet → Advanced network settings → More adapter options
Right-click your connection → Properties → IPv4 → Advanced → WINS tab
Disable 'File and Printer Sharing' if not needed
Run: sudo systemctl stop nmbd smbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable nmbd smbd
Disable Samba unless you intentionally share files on the LAN

143.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted IMAP. Credentials and email can be sniffed. Prefer IMAPS on port 993.

steps: In your mail server admin UI, disable IMAP or require TLS
Stop mail services you do not need via services.msc
Configure Dovecot for SSL-only or stop: sudo systemctl stop dovecot
Use a hosted mail provider instead of self-hosting when possible

389.dangerous_reason: Directory services often carry credentials and user data. Unencrypted LDAP can leak authentication traffic; restrict to trusted management hosts.

steps: Block inbound 389 from non-domain / guest networks in Windows Firewall
Do not expose Active Directory to the internet

linux.description: Stop or bind LDAP on Linux
steps: Stop OpenLDAP if unused: sudo systemctl stop slapd && sudo systemctl disable slapd
If required, bind to localhost or VPN-only interfaces
Only run directory services on dedicated, segmented hosts

443.dangerous_reason: Encrypted web server. If unintended, stop the service. If legitimate, restrict with firewall rules and keep software patched (Heartbleed CVE-2014-0160 affected TLS stacks).

windows.description: Manage HTTPS web server on Windows
steps: If IIS is unintended: disable 'World Wide Web Publishing Service'
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → restrict inbound 443 to trusted networks
Keep Windows and IIS fully updated
If unintended: sudo systemctl stop nginx apache2
Run: sudo systemctl disable nginx (or apache2)
If needed: bind to specific IPs and use firewall (ufw allow from LAN only)
If unintended web server: sudo systemctl stop nginx apache2
Use ufw to allow 443 only from your LAN subnet
Do not expose admin panels to the internet without authentication

445.dangerous_reason: Primary ransomware vector. WannaCry (MS17-010/EternalBlue) and NotPetya exploited SMB. Should never be exposed beyond a trusted LAN.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Settings → Network → disable 'File and Printer Sharing' on public networks
Run: sudo systemctl stop smbd nmbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable smbd nmbd
Verify: sudo systemctl status smbd (should show 'inactive')
Run: sudo systemctl stop smbd nmbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable smbd nmbd

465.dangerous_reason: SMTP over TLS submission. Legitimate for mail servers, but unexpected listeners may indicate malware relays or misconfigured mail stacks.

steps: Confirm whether a mail server should be running
In mail server settings, restrict relay and require authentication
Block inbound 465 from the internet at the router

linux.description: Harden or stop SMTPS on Linux
steps: If unused: sudo systemctl stop postfix && sudo systemctl disable postfix
If needed: require auth, disable open relay, keep TLS current

pi.description: Stop unexpected SMTPS on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop Postfix/Exim if you are not running mail
Never allow an open relay on a home Pi

548.dangerous_reason: Apple Filing Protocol file sharing. Legacy AFP can expose shared folders; prefer SMB with strong auth or turn sharing off when unused.

steps: AFP is uncommon on Windows — check third-party file servers
Stop any AppleTalk/AFP service or Docker container publishing 548
Prefer Samba (SMB) with authentication if file sharing is required

pi.description: Disable AFP on Raspberry Pi / macOS shares
steps: On Pi with Netatalk: sudo systemctl stop netatalk && sudo systemctl disable netatalk
On Mac: System Settings → General → Sharing → turn off File Sharing if unused

554.dangerous_reason: IP camera and video streaming. If unintended, stop the service — exposed streams leak live video. Default camera credentials are brute-forced constantly.

windows.description: Secure or disable RTSP on Windows
steps: Identify the camera app or NVR using port 554
Change all default admin passwords on cameras
Disable UPnP and remote access in camera settings
Do not port-forward 554 from your router
Stop the service: sudo systemctl stop mediamtx (or your NVR service)
Keep cameras on a separate VLAN or guest network
Stop camera streaming service: sudo systemctl stop <rtsp-service>
Use local viewing only — do not expose Pi cameras to the internet

587.dangerous_reason: Mail submission port. Expected on mail servers; unexpected listeners may be spam relays. Require authentication and TLS.

steps: Block internet inbound 587 unless you intentionally host mail

linux.description: Harden or stop submission on Linux
steps: If used: enable SASL auth, restrict networks, no open relay

pi.description: Stop unexpected submission on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop mail services unless you operate a mail server
Use your ISP/provider SMTP instead for home use

631.dangerous_reason: Internet Printing Protocol. Exposed printers can leak documents, allow job injection, or be used for lateral movement.

steps: Disable 'File and Printer Sharing' for guest networks

linux.description: Stop CUPS on Linux if unused
steps: Or edit /etc/cups/cupsd.conf to Listen localhost:631 only
Run: sudo systemctl stop cups && sudo systemctl disable cups if unused
If printing is needed, bind CUPS to LAN only and require auth
Isolate printers on an IoT VLAN when possible

636.dangerous_reason: LDAP over TLS. Safer than plain LDAP but still a high-value directory endpoint — restrict to domain controllers / admin networks.

steps: Allow 636 only from domain-joined / admin subnets
Block from guest/IoT VLANs in Windows Firewall

linux.description: Stop or restrict LDAPS on Linux
steps: If required, firewall to management hosts only

pi.description: Avoid LDAPS on home Raspberry Pi
steps: Do not run directory services on a home Pi exposed to all LAN clients

873.dangerous_reason: File sync daemon. If unintended, stop rsync — misconfigured rsync modules allow unauthenticated or weakly authenticated file access and data exfiltration.

steps: Rsync on Windows usually runs via Cygwin, WSL, or cwRsync
Stop the rsync daemon process or Windows service
Remove rsync from startup if not intentionally used
If using rsync for backups, bind to localhost or require SSH tunnel only

993.dangerous_reason: Encrypted IMAP. Usually legitimate for mail, but unexpected open IMAPS may mean an unintended mail stack is reachable.

steps: Confirm the mail server should be listening
Do not expose 993 to the internet unless required
Keep TLS certificates valid; disable weak ciphers

pi.description: Stop unexpected IMAPS on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop mail daemons if you are not hosting email

995.dangerous_reason: Encrypted POP3. Prefer disabling unused mail protocols; unexpected listeners should be investigated.

steps: Disable POP3S in mail server if clients use IMAP only
Disable POP3 in Dovecot protocols= line or stop dovecot

1433.dangerous_reason: Extremely valuable target for ransomware and data theft. Should NEVER be exposed to the internet. SQL injection and brute-force attacks are automated at scale.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: SQL Server Configuration Manager → disable TCP/IP protocol
Block port 1433 in Windows Firewall for all public networks
Verify: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :1433 (should return nothing)
MSSQL is rarely needed on Pi — use SQLite for local projects

1521.dangerous_reason: Critical enterprise database. Should NEVER be exposed. Contains sensitive business data — targeted by APT groups and ransomware (CVE-2021-44228 Log4j affected Oracle middleware stacks).

steps: Right-click each → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Run: sudo systemctl stop oracle-xe (or oracledb)
Oracle is not typically run on Pi
If a listener is open: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :1521
Stop and disable the unexpected service immediately
Use SQLite or PostgreSQL bound to localhost for Pi projects

1720.dangerous_reason: Legacy video conferencing signaling. Rarely needed at home; exposed H.323 stacks have a history of remote flaws.

steps: Stop Asterisk/FreeSWITCH modules using H.323 if unused
Prefer SIP over TLS on modern stacks
Stop PBX containers/services if not intentionally running VoIP

1723.dangerous_reason: Obsolete VPN protocol with known cryptographic weaknesses. Prefer WireGuard or OpenVPN/IKEv2 instead.

steps: Remove PPTP VPN connections you do not use
Do not enable Routing and Remote Access PPTP
Run: sudo systemctl stop pptpd && sudo systemctl disable pptpd
Use WireGuard for home VPN access

1883.dangerous_reason: IoT messaging broker. Often left without authentication — attackers can subscribe/publish to home automation topics.

steps: If running Mosquitto/EMQX in Docker/WSL, require username/password
Edit /etc/mosquitto/mosquitto.conf: allow_anonymous false and set password_file
Or stop if unused: sudo systemctl stop mosquitto && sudo systemctl disable mosquitto
Isolate IoT brokers on a separate VLAN when possible

1900.dangerous_reason: Universal Plug and Play can automatically open firewall holes. Abused in DDoS amplification attacks and Mirai botnet propagation on IoT devices.

windows.description: Disable UPnP on Windows and router
steps: Log into your router admin page and disable UPnP
Windows Settings → Network → disable 'Network Discovery' on public networks
Do not allow apps to automatically configure port forwarding
Disable UPnP on your router (most important step)
Disable UPnP in your router settings
Do not run UPnP services on Pi unless absolutely required

2049.dangerous_reason: Allows remote file mounts over the network. Misconfigured exports permit unauthenticated access — used in lateral movement and data theft on Linux networks.

steps: Uncheck 'Services for NFS' and 'Client for NFS'
Press Win+R, type 'services.msc' → disable 'Server for NFS' if present
Use SMB or SFTP instead for file sharing
Edit /etc/exports — remove all export lines or comment them out
Use SSH/SFTP for file access instead

2222.dangerous_reason: Alternate SSH port. Same risk as port 22 — brute force and key theft. Confirm it is your intentional SSH listener.

steps: services.msc → stop OpenSSH SSH Server if unused
Stop ssh if not needed, or change back to 22 behind fail2ban

pi.description: Stop alternate SSH on Raspberry Pi
steps: Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config and remove alternate Port lines if unwanted
Use key-only auth and disable PasswordAuthentication

2375.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted Docker Engine API. Anyone who can reach it can start privileged containers and often take over the host.

windows.description: Disable Docker TCP API on Windows
steps: Docker Desktop → Settings → General/Docker Engine: remove hosts tcp://0.0.0.0:2375
Do not expose Docker over TCP without TLS
Remove -H tcp://0.0.0.0:2375 from systemd docker.service override
sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl restart docker

pi.description: Disable Docker TCP on Raspberry Pi
steps: Same as Linux — never leave 2375 open on the LAN

2376.dangerous_reason: Docker API with TLS. Safer than 2375 but still full engine control — restrict to admin hosts and protect client certificates.

windows.description: Restrict Docker TLS API on Windows
steps: Confirm Docker Desktop/engine TLS settings are intentional
Firewall allowlist management IPs for TCP 2376
Store TLS keys with strict permissions
Prefer SSH jump access instead of LAN-wide Docker API

pi.description: Restrict Docker TLS on Raspberry Pi
steps: Avoid LAN-wide Docker API even with TLS on a home Pi

3000.dangerous_reason: Common development server port (Node/React). Dev servers often lack auth and expose source maps or admin UIs.

steps: Check Task Manager for node.exe listening on 3000
Do not bind dev servers to 0.0.0.0 on a shared LAN
Kill the node/python process or stop the systemd unit

pi.description: Stop dev servers on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop the app service or kill the process
Use a reverse proxy with auth for anything shared on LAN

3306.dangerous_reason: Relational database with sensitive data. Exposed instances are scanned and brute-forced constantly. GDPR and credential databases are common exfiltration targets.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SQLite for Pi projects unless MySQL is essential

3389.dangerous_reason: RDP brute-force and credential stuffing are among the top attack types (BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708 affected older systems). Exposed RDP gives full desktop control.

steps: Select 'Don't allow remote connections to this computer'
Settings → System → Remote Desktop → toggle Off
Most Linux systems do not run RDP by default
Pi uses VNC (5900), not RDP, by default
If xrdp installed: sudo systemctl stop xrdp && sudo systemctl disable xrdp
Disable VNC via: sudo raspi-config → Interface Options → VNC → Disable

5000.dangerous_reason: Often Flask/UPnP related. Unauthenticated web apps or discovery services can leak device control APIs.

steps: Stop Flask/Docker/UPnP software you do not need
Disable UPnP on the router if unwanted
Stop the Flask app or Docker publish
Disable UPnP clients if not required
Avoid binding personal apps to 0.0.0.0 without a password

5001.dangerous_reason: Common Synology DSM alternate HTTPS/admin port. Admin panels are high-value targets — use strong MFA and updates.

windows.description: Secure Synology access from Windows LAN
steps: Disable QuickConnect if unused; restrict admin to trusted IPs
Change default ports only as defense-in-depth, not instead of MFA

linux.description: Secure Synology from Linux admin hosts
steps: Disable unused packages and SSH if not required

pi.description: Secure Synology via Pi network controls
steps: Put NAS on a trusted VLAN

5432.dangerous_reason: PostgreSQL database. Should NEVER be exposed to the internet. Automated scans exploit weak passwords and misconfigured pg_hba.conf for full data access.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Bind to localhost only if your project requires PostgreSQL

5555.dangerous_reason: Android Debug Bridge. If reachable on the LAN, attackers can install apps, exfiltrate data, or take shell control of the phone/TV.

steps: On the Android device: Developer options → turn off USB/network debugging
Disable wireless debugging on the device
Do not run adb tcpip 5555 on untrusted networks

pi.description: Disable ADB on Raspberry Pi / Android TV
steps: Turn off Developer mode / ADB on the TV/box

5672.dangerous_reason: RabbitMQ / message broker port. Unauthenticated brokers allow queue takeover and lateral movement into app backends.

steps: If using RabbitMQ/Docker, require users and strong passwords
Do not publish 5672 to the whole LAN without need
Enable RabbitMQ auth; delete guest remote access
Stop if unused: sudo systemctl stop rabbitmq-server

pi.description: Stop AMQP on Raspberry Pi if unused
steps: sudo systemctl stop rabbitmq-server && sudo systemctl disable rabbitmq-server
Prefer managed brokers for production apps

5900.dangerous_reason: Remote desktop without adequate encryption by default on many implementations. Exposed VNC allows screen viewing and keyboard control — common brute-force target.

steps: Press Win+R, type 'services.msc' → stop VNC Server service
For RealVNC: sudo systemctl stop vncserver-x11-serviced

5901.dangerous_reason: Additional VNC display. Same risk as 5900 — remote screen control, often weak passwords.

steps: Stop vncserver / tigervnc units for display :1
Prefer SSH port-forwarding or remote desktop with strong auth
sudo systemctl stop vncserver-x11-serviced or wayvnc if unused
Disable in raspi-config → Interface Options → VNC
Never use a blank VNC password

5984.dangerous_reason: NoSQL document database. Exposed CouchDB with admin party mode allows all data to be read and written without authentication — mass data breaches occurred in 2017.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
If running in Docker: docker stop <couchdb-container>
Edit /opt/couchdb/etc/local.ini — bind to 127.0.0.1 only if needed locally
Never expose CouchDB to the internet without authentication enabled

5985.dangerous_reason: Windows Remote Management over HTTP. Powerful remote admin channel — restrict to admin subnets and prefer HTTPS (5986).

steps: Run: winrm quickconfig only if you need it
Disable WinRM if unused: Stop-Service WinRM; Set-Service WinRM -StartupType Disabled
Block TCP 5985 to Windows hosts from guest networks
Manage Windows only from trusted jump hosts
Do not allow IoT VLAN access to 5985/5986
Keep Windows admin on a trusted segment

5986.dangerous_reason: WinRM over HTTPS. Encrypted but still full remote admin — treat as a privileged service.

steps: Require domain auth / strong local admin passwords
Disable if remote management is unused
Audit successful WinRM logons on Windows Event Log
Block 5986 from untrusted VLANs at the router/Pi firewall

6379.dangerous_reason: In-memory cache/database with no authentication by default. Complete data exposure and remote code execution via CONFIG command — used to deploy malware at scale.

steps: Set Startup type to Disabled → Stop
If in Docker: docker stop <redis-container> publishing 6379
Bind to localhost only if a local app needs Redis

8000.dangerous_reason: Alternate web/dev port. Often unauthenticated apps, proxies, or cameras.

steps: Do not expose admin UIs without a password
Stop the systemd unit or container
Stop the app or move behind authenticated reverse proxy

8008.dangerous_reason: Alternate HTTP. Common for proxies, Chromecast/Google services, or misbound apps.

steps: Stop if not required for a known app

8009.dangerous_reason: Apache JServ Protocol. Historically abused (Ghostcat). Should not be exposed beyond the app server backend.

steps: Edit Tomcat server.xml and remove the AJP Connector
Never publish 8009 to the LAN if avoidable

8080.dangerous_reason: Alternative HTTP port with the same risk as port 80. Admin panels, proxies, and dev servers often use default credentials on this port.

steps: Note the PID → Task Manager → Details → end process
Or disable the service in services.msc (Tomcat, Jenkins, etc.)
Do not port-forward 8080 from your router
Run: sudo systemctl stop <service-name> (tomcat, jenkins, etc.)
Bind dev servers to 127.0.0.1 only during development

pi.description: Stop HTTP alt on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop the web dashboard or IoT service

8081.dangerous_reason: Alternate HTTP admin/proxy port. Frequently used by cameras, reverse proxies, and panels with default credentials.

steps: Change default passwords or stop the service
Require auth on any remaining UI
Stop the service; isolate cameras on IoT VLAN

8443.dangerous_reason: Alternative HTTPS port. Stop if unintended. If legitimate, restrict with firewall rules and enforce strong authentication on admin interfaces.

steps: Stop the application in services.msc or Task Manager
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → block inbound 8443 on public networks
Change default admin passwords on any device using this port
Use ufw: sudo ufw deny 8443 or allow from LAN only

pi.description: Stop HTTPS alt on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop and disable the listening service
Use VPN or local access instead of exposing 8443 to the internet

8883.dangerous_reason: MQTT over TLS. Better than plain MQTT, but still needs authentication and access control lists.

steps: Configure Mosquitto listener 8883 with certs and password_file
Do not expose 8883 to the internet

8888.dangerous_reason: Alternate web port used by notebooks, proxies, and admin tools. Often lacks authentication in lab setups.

steps: Add token/password auth if you must keep it
Use SSH tunnels instead of LAN exposure

9000.dangerous_reason: Often Portainer, SonarQube, or similar admin UIs. Full container/app control if credentials are weak.

steps: Set strong admin password; enable SSO if available
Stop container if unused: docker stop portainer
Do not leave Portainer open to the whole home LAN

9090.dangerous_reason: Prometheus and other admin UIs. Metrics can leak infrastructure detail; UIs may allow config changes.

steps: Add auth proxy or stop if unused
Put Prometheus behind authenticated reverse proxy
Or stop: sudo systemctl stop prometheus
Never expose Prometheus to the internet without auth

9100.dangerous_reason: Raw printer port. If you do not have a network printer, disable it. Allows print job injection, firmware attacks, and lateral movement on corporate networks.

steps: Settings → Bluetooth & devices → Printers → disable sharing if not needed
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → block port 9100 on public/guest networks
Update printer firmware from the manufacturer website
Do not port-forward 9100 from your router
Edit /etc/cups/cupsd.conf — restrict Listen and Allow to localhost or LAN
Do not expose printer ports to the internet
If Pi shares a printer via CUPS, restrict cupsd.conf to LAN only

9200.dangerous_reason: Search and indexing engine. If exposed, all indexed data — often logs with credentials and PII — is readable. Ransomware groups target open Elasticsearch clusters.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Edit elasticsearch.yml: network.host: 127.0.0.1 and enable xpack security
Elasticsearch is heavy for Pi — use local SQLite or bind to localhost only

9443.dangerous_reason: Alternate HTTPS admin port (Portainer HTTPS, appliances). Treat as a privileged management endpoint.

10000.dangerous_reason: Webmin system administration UI. Full root-equivalent control of the host if compromised.

steps: Webmin is uncommon on Windows — check WSL/VM
Stop the service and uninstall if not needed
If kept: change port, force SSL, restrict by IP, use strong credentials
sudo systemctl stop webmin && sudo systemctl disable webmin
Prefer SSH with keys for Pi administration

11211.dangerous_reason: In-memory cache often without authentication. Abused for data exposure and DDoS amplification when reachable.

steps: sudo systemctl stop memcached && sudo systemctl disable memcached
Bind to localhost only if you must keep it

15672.dangerous_reason: RabbitMQ management UI. Default guest credentials are a common compromise path.

steps: Create admin user with strong password
Stop if unused: sudo systemctl stop rabbitmq-server
Do not expose 15672 on the home LAN without a strong password

25565.dangerous_reason: Minecraft server. Unpatched servers are frequently exploited; also attracts internet scanners when port-forwarded.

steps: Set online-mode=true and a strong whitelist
Firewall to friends' IPs when possible
Stop: systemctl stop minecraft if unused
Be aware Pi servers are often outdated — update often or stop the service

27017.dangerous_reason: NoSQL database. Older versions had no authentication by default — thousands of databases were wiped in 2017. Full read/write access if exposed without auth.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SQLite for Pi projects unless MongoDB is essential

32400.dangerous_reason: Plex Media Server. Exposes library metadata and streaming; weak accounts or remote access misconfig can leak media and tokens.

steps: Plex settings → Remote Access: disable if unused
Use a strong Plex account password + 2FA
Disable remote access if only LAN streaming is needed
Disable remote access for home-only use
Put media servers on a trusted VLAN

50070.dangerous_reason: Hadoop HDFS NameNode web UI. If exposed, attackers can browse and manipulate the entire data lake — used in big-data exfiltration and ransomware campaigns.

steps: Hadoop on Windows is uncommon — check WSL or Docker
Stop Hadoop services or Docker containers publishing 50070
Run stop-all.sh or stop-dfs.sh in Hadoop install directory if present
Disable Hadoop services from starting at boot
Bind NameNode to localhost only in hdfs-site.xml if needed locally
Hadoop is rarely run on Pi in production
Stop the Hadoop or Docker service immediately
Do not expose big-data cluster ports to the internet

62078.dangerous_reason: Apple lockdownd / sync related service seen on iOS devices. Usually expected on phones; treat unexpected listeners on non-Apple hosts as suspicious.

steps: If seen on a PC, identify the process with netstat -ano | findstr :62078
Otherwise ignore when the device is a known iPhone/iPad
Confirm the host is an Apple device in inventory
If a Linux host listens here, investigate the process and stop it
Expected on iPhones — no action if ownership is confirmed
Isolate unknown devices that expose unusual high ports
